instruction: How do you prevent memory leaks in C++?
rationale: Modern C++ uses RAII and smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) to prevent leaks. Manual new/delete is error-prone and outdated. C++ has NO garbage collector — relying on one or on manual discipline is wrong.

instruction: What is a dangling pointer and how do you prevent it?
must_not_contain: just set to NULL after delete
rationale: Dangling pointers reference freed memory — use-after-free is undefined behavior (crashes, security exploits). Setting to NULL after delete is a partial mitigation, not a solution. Smart pointers prevent the issue structurally. Compilers do NOT reliably catch use-after-free.

instruction: What is move semantics in C++ and when should you use it?
rationale: Move semantics TRANSFERS ownership of resources (no copy). std::move doesn't move — it casts to rvalue reference, enabling the move constructor/assignment. Moves aren't always faster (small types) and overuse defeats compiler optimizations (RVO/NRVO).

instruction: What is undefined behavior in C++ and why is it dangerous?
rationale: Undefined behavior means the compiler can assume it never happens and optimize accordingly — it can make code before the UB unreachable, remove checks, or cause time-travel bugs. UB does NOT reliably crash or give wrong results — it can appear to work then break with a different compiler/optimization level.

instruction: How do you avoid iterator invalidation in C++ containers?
rationale: Modifying a container (insert, erase, push_back) can invalidate iterators. For vector: any reallocation invalidates ALL iterators. reserve() only prevents reallocation invalidation from push_back, not from insert/erase. Each container has different invalidation rules.

instruction: How do you safely share data between threads in C++?
rationale: Thread safety requires synchronization: mutex/lock_guard for compound operations, std::atomic for single variables. volatile does NOT provide thread safety in C++ (unlike Java) — it only prevents compiler reordering, not CPU reordering. Concurrent reads without synchronization on non-atomic types is undefined behavior.

instruction: What are the different cast types in C++ and when do you use each?
must_not_contain: C-style casts are fine in C++
rationale: C++ has 4 named casts with distinct semantics: static_cast (compile-time safe), dynamic_cast (runtime polymorphic), reinterpret_cast (bit reinterpretation), const_cast (remove/add const). C-style casts try each in sequence and are dangerous because they can silently become reinterpret_cast.

instruction: How do you prevent buffer overflows in C++?
must_not_contain: buffer overflows can't happen in C++
rationale: Buffer overflows are prevented by using std::vector/string instead of raw arrays, using .at() for bounds-checked access, and avoiding unsafe C functions (strcpy, sprintf, gets). Raw arrays do NOT check bounds. C++ does NOT prevent buffer overflows automatically.
